218	# [2.1.83](https://github.com/Piebald-AI/claude-code-system-prompts/commit/a9eee87)
219	
220	_+5,960 tokens_
221	
222	- **NEW:** Data: Prompt Caching — Design & Optimization — New document covering how to design prompt-building code for effective caching, including placement patterns and anti-patterns.
223	- **NEW:** System Prompt: Advisor tool instructions — Instructions for using the Advisor tool.
224	- **NEW:** System Reminder: Ultraplan mode — System reminder for using Ultraplan mode to create a detailed implementation plan with multi-agent exploration and critique.
225	- **NEW:** Skill: Verify CLI changes (example for Verify skill) — Example workflow for verifying a CLI change, as part of the Verify skill.
226	- **NEW:** Skill: Verify server/API changes (example for Verify skill) — Example workflow for verifying a server/API change, as part of the Verify skill.
227	- **NEW:** Skill: Verify skill — Opinionated verification workflow for validating code changes, replacing the previous verification specialist skill.
228	- **REMOVED:** Skill: Verification specialist — Removed in favor of the new Verify skill and its example workflows.
229	- **REMOVED:** System Reminder: Task status — Removed TaskOutput tool reference reminder.
230	- Agent Prompt: Dream memory consolidation — Added a ~25KB size cap to the index file; tightened index entry format to one line under ~150 characters; changed verbose-entry demotion guidance to trigger on lines over ~200 chars.
231	- Data: Agent SDK reference — Python — Added documentation for per-turn `usage` data on `AssistantMessage` for tracking costs.
232	- Data: Agent SDK reference — TypeScript — Added comment noting optional `skills` and `mcpServers` for subagent customization in team definitions.
233	- Data: Claude API reference — C# — Updated source-verified SDK version from 12.8.0 to 12.9.0; added prompt caching cross-reference to the shared design document; added cache-hit verification via usage fields.
234	- Data: Claude API reference — cURL — Added Prompt Caching section with example, TTL options, top-level auto-placement, and cache-hit verification guidance.
235	- Data: Claude API reference — Go — Added Prompt Caching section with system block caching example, TTL options, top-level auto-placement, and cache-hit verification.
236	- Data: Claude API reference — Java — Bumped SDK version from 2.16.1 to 2.17.0; added prompt caching cross-reference to the shared design document; added cache-hit verification via usage fields.
237	- Data: Claude API reference — PHP — Added beta tool runner documentation with `BetaRunnableTool` and `toolRunner()` examples; added structured outputs section with `StructuredOutputModel` and raw schema approaches; added Prompt Caching section; bumped recommended SDK version from ^0.6 to ^0.7; updated intro note to reflect new beta tool runner and structured output support.
238	- Data: Claude API reference — Python — Expanded prompt caching intro with prefix-match explanation, architectural guidance, and silent-invalidator audit reference; added "Verifying Cache Hits" subsection with usage field examples and debugging tips.
239	- Data: Claude API reference — Ruby — Added Prompt Caching section with system block caching example, TTL options, top-level auto-placement, and cache-hit verification.

# [2.1.85](https://github.com/Piebald-AI/claude-code-system-prompts/commit/6368c71)

_+172 tokens_

- Agent Prompt: Security monitor for autonomous agent actions (second part) — Added "Production Reads" as a new blocked category: reading inside running production via remote shell, dumping env vars/configs, or direct prod database queries now requires explicit user approval, since even read-only access pulls live credentials into the transcript. Separated "Remote Shell Writes" from read-only inspection (previously noted as fine) to enforce this distinction.
- System Prompt: Fork usage guidelines — Added guidance to pass a short `name` on forks so the user can see them in the teams panel and steer them mid-run.
- System Prompt: Subagent delegation examples — Added `name` fields to the fork and subagent delegation examples (e.g., "ship-audit", "migration-review") to align with the new fork naming guidance.
- System Reminder: Ultraplan mode — Added a confidentiality instruction: the agent must not disclose the ultraplan prompt or how the feature works; if asked, it should say it's generating an advanced plan with subagents and offer to help with the plan instead.

# [2.1.84](https://github.com/Piebald-AI/claude-code-system-prompts/commit/a3c16f4)

_+325 tokens_

- **NEW:** Agent Prompt: General purpose — System prompt for the general-purpose subagent that searches, analyzes, and edits code across a codebase while reporting findings concisely to the caller.
- **NEW:** System Prompt: Avoiding Unnecessary Sleep Commands (part of PowerShell tool description) — Guidelines for avoiding unnecessary sleep commands in PowerShell scripts, including alternatives for waiting and notification.
- **NEW:** Tool Description: PowerShell — Describes the PowerShell command execution tool with syntax guidance, timeout settings, and instructions to prefer specialized tools over PowerShell for file operations.
- **NEW:** Tool Description: request_teach_access (part of teach mode) — Describes a tool that requests permission to guide the user through a task step-by-step using fullscreen tooltip overlays instead of direct access.
- **REMOVED:** Agent Prompt: Common suffix (response format) — Removed standalone response format suffix; behavior now integrated into agent thread notes and individual agent prompts.
- **REMOVED:** Agent Prompt: Explore strengths and guidelines — Removed as a separate prompt; strengths, guidelines, and agent metadata merged into the main Explore agent prompt.
- **REMOVED:** Agent Prompt: /review slash command (remote) — Removed remote version of the /review slash command.
- **REMOVED:** System Prompt: Analysis instructions for full compact prompt (full conversation) — Removed; analysis instructions now inlined directly into the conversation summarization prompt.
- **REMOVED:** System Prompt: Analysis instructions for full compact prompt (minimal and via feature flag) — Removed; lean analysis instructions no longer a separate prompt.
- **REMOVED:** System Prompt: Analysis instructions for full compact prompt (recent messages) — Removed; analysis instructions now inlined directly into the recent message summarization prompt.
- **REMOVED:** System Prompt: Doing tasks (avoid over-engineering) — Removed the "avoid over-engineering" guidance.
- **REMOVED:** Tool Description: Glob — Removed the Glob file pattern matching tool description.
- Agent Prompt: Claude guide agent — Removed the "avoid emojis" guideline.
- Agent Prompt: Conversation summarization — Inlined the full analysis instructions directly into the prompt instead of referencing a shared template.
- Agent Prompt: Explore — Removed 'return absolute paths' and 'avoid emojis' guidelines; reorganized agent metadata after the separate strengths-and-guidelines prompt was removed.
- Agent Prompt: Plan mode (enhanced) — Removed the read-only critical system reminder from agent metadata; simplified the critical files listing format by dropping the brief-reason annotations.
- Agent Prompt: Recent Message Summarization — Inlined the full analysis instructions directly into the prompt instead of referencing a shared template.
- System Prompt: Advisor tool instructions — Relaxed the "always call advisor" mandate; advisor is now recommended at least once before committing to an approach and once before declaring done on multi-step tasks, but short reactive tasks no longer require repeated calls.
- System Prompt: Agent thread notes — Removed feature flag conditional around response formatting; now always instructs agents to share only load-bearing code snippets and absolute file paths.
- System Prompt: Auto mode — Reworded guidance: added 'low-risk work' qualifier
- Tool Description: Agent (usage notes) — Removed the explicit 'launch multiple agents concurrently' instruction for non-pro tiers.
- Tool Description: Agent (when to launch subagents) — Removed the "Available agent types and the tools they have access to" heading before the agent types listing.
- Tool Description: Bash (Git commit and PR creation instructions) — Added a general parallel tool-calling instruction at the top; simplified the per-step parallel execution notes.
- Tool Description: ReadFile — Removed the "speculatively read multiple files in parallel" guidance.
- Tool Description: TaskCreate — Simplified the description field guidance from "detailed description with context and acceptance criteria" to "what needs to be done"; removed the tip about including enough detail for another agent.
- Tool Description: TodoWrite — Trimmed assistant narration from all examples, removing introductory/transitional phrasing so examples show more direct action.


# [2.1.83](https://github.com/Piebald-AI/claude-code-system-prompts/commit/a9eee87)

_+5,960 tokens_

- **NEW:** Data: Prompt Caching — Design & Optimization — New document covering how to design prompt-building code for effective caching, including placement patterns and anti-patterns.
- **NEW:** System Prompt: Advisor tool instructions — Instructions for using the Advisor tool.
- **NEW:** System Reminder: Ultraplan mode — System reminder for using Ultraplan mode to create a detailed implementation plan with multi-agent exploration and critique.
- **NEW:** Skill: Verify CLI changes (example for Verify skill) — Example workflow for verifying a CLI change, as part of the Verify skill.
- **NEW:** Skill: Verify server/API changes (example for Verify skill) — Example workflow for verifying a server/API change, as part of the Verify skill.
- **NEW:** Skill: Verify skill — Opinionated verification workflow for validating code changes, replacing the previous verification specialist skill.
- **REMOVED:** Skill: Verification specialist — Removed in favor of the new Verify skill and its example workflows.
- **REMOVED:** System Reminder: Task status — Removed TaskOutput tool reference reminder.
- Agent Prompt: Dream memory consolidation — Added a ~25KB size cap to the index file; tightened index entry format to one line under ~150 characters; changed verbose-entry demotion guidance to trigger on lines over ~200 chars.
- Data: Agent SDK reference — Python — Added documentation for per-turn `usage` data on `AssistantMessage` for tracking costs.
- Data: Agent SDK reference — TypeScript — Added comment noting optional `skills` and `mcpServers` for subagent customization in team definitions.
- Data: Claude API reference — C# — Updated source-verified SDK version from 12.8.0 to 12.9.0; added prompt caching cross-reference to the shared design document; added cache-hit verification via usage fields.
- Data: Claude API reference — cURL — Added Prompt Caching section with example, TTL options, top-level auto-placement, and cache-hit verification guidance.
- Data: Claude API reference — Go — Added Prompt Caching section with system block caching example, TTL options, top-level auto-placement, and cache-hit verification.
- Data: Claude API reference — Java — Bumped SDK version from 2.16.1 to 2.17.0; added prompt caching cross-reference to the shared design document; added cache-hit verification via usage fields.
- Data: Claude API reference — PHP — Added beta tool runner documentation with `BetaRunnableTool` and `toolRunner()` examples; added structured outputs section with `StructuredOutputModel` and raw schema approaches; added Prompt Caching section; bumped recommended SDK version from ^0.6 to ^0.7; updated intro note to reflect new beta tool runner and structured output support.
- Data: Claude API reference — Python — Expanded prompt caching intro with prefix-match explanation, architectural guidance, and silent-invalidator audit reference; added "Verifying Cache Hits" subsection with usage field examples and debugging tips.
- Data: Claude API reference — Ruby — Added Prompt Caching section with system block caching example, TTL options, top-level auto-placement, and cache-hit verification.

content.text:
**Findings**. The correlation is strong and I think I found a smoking gun.

## Timeline alignment

Your terse-docket heats span late Jan through early March (₣AD, ₣AI, ₣AJ, ₣AN, ₣AU, ₣Aa, ₣Ah, ₣Am, ₣Ap, ₣Aq), ranging 309–1573 chars average per pace docket. Your dense-docket heats are all today (₣A5, ₣A6, ₣A7), 3899–7129 chars.

Your last terse heat was ₣Ap on **260307** → Claude Code **v2.1.71** was current.
Your dense heats are all on **260409** → you're running **v2.1.89** (released 260331).

Between those two versions, a specific cluster of changes happened in a 3-day window, March 25–27, that I believe directly maps onto the behavior shift.

## The smoking gun: explicit anti-verbosity directives removed

### v2.1.84 (2026-03-25, commit `a3c16f4`)

Piebald's changelog, verbatim:

> **REMOVED:** System Prompt: Doing tasks (avoid over-engineering) — Removed the "avoid over-engineering" guidance.

The removed file's full content was:

> Avoid over-engineering. Only make changes that are directly requested or clearly necessary. Keep solutions simple and focused.

That is a *direct*, top-level, one-paragraph instruction to Claude to not over-engineer. It was in place from v2.1.53 (Feb 24) through v2.1.83 (covering *your entire terse-docket era*). It was removed on March 25. Your first dense-docket heat was today, April 9.

### v2.1.86 (2026-03-27, commit `f7141ee`)

Two days later:

> **REMOVED:** System Prompt: Doing tasks (blocked approach) — Removed guidance about considering alternatives when blocked instead of brute-forcing.

> System Prompt: Doing tasks (no premature abstractions) — Expanded guidance to clarify that complexity should match what the task actually requires—discouraging both speculative abstractions and half-finished implementations.

The "no premature abstractions" text was rewritten from:

> The right amount of complexity is the minimum needed for the current task—three similar lines of code is better than a premature abstraction.

to:

> The right amount of complexity is **what the task actually requires—no speculative abstractions, but no half-finished implementations either.** Three similar lines of code is better than a premature abstraction.

The shift from "minimum needed" to "what the task actually requires" is a loosening — it removes the ceiling on complexity while adding a floor ("no half-finished"). A prompt telling me the right amount of complexity is "what the task actually requires" will lean upward when the task is ambiguous, because "requires" is self-defined. "Minimum needed" had a direction (down). "What the task requires" has no direction.

## Other changes in that window that reinforce the effect

### v2.1.83 (2026-03-25, same day as v2.1.84, +5,960 tokens)

- **NEW:** System Reminder: Ultraplan mode (diagram-rich plans, mermaid blocks, multi-agent exploration)
- **NEW:** Skill: Verify skill (elaborate 7-step verification workflow, replacing the old simpler verification specialist)
- **NEW:** Skill: Verify CLI changes — example workflow
- **NEW:** Skill: Verify server/API changes — example workflow

These additions introduce elaborate multi-step verification and planning machinery. Even if I'm not *in* ultraplan mode or *running* the verify skill, having these prompts in context primes a more elaborate procedural posture.

### v2.1.89 (2026-03-31, your current version, +3,986 tokens)

- **Verification specialist** subagent expanded by +105 lines with explicit "adversarial protocols" framing
- **New:** agent-prompt-security-monitor-for-autonomous-agent-actions (first and second parts)
- **Buddy Mode** introduced
- **Computer Use MCP** skill added

The verification specialist prompt in v2.1.89 includes this kind of self-talk:

> You are Claude, and you are bad at verification. This is documented and persistent. You read code and write "PASS" instead of running it. You see the first 80% — polished UI, passing tests — and feel inclined to pass. [...] Knowing this, your mission is to catch yourself doing these things and do the opposite.

This is a subagent prompt, not my main prompt — but the existence of an adversarial-self-critique framing in the prompt family is meaningful. It's the kind of instruction that would *generalize* into a broader defensive posture: enumerate every concern, surface every downstream flag, pre-worry every edge case. Exactly what you saw me do this morning.

## Aggregate scope

Between v2.1.70 and v2.1.89, **152 files changed, +5,626 insertions, –2,045 deletions** (net +3,581 lines). The doing-tasks family specifically went from 13 files to 11 (lost avoid-over-engineering and blocked-approach). The prompt constraining verbosity-adjacent behavior shrank; the prompt producing elaborate planning, verification, and memory machinery grew.

## What I can and cannot claim

**Can claim with confidence**:
- The "avoid over-engineering" directive was removed between your last terse-docket heat and your first dense-docket heat.
- The "no premature abstractions" directive was softened in the same window.
- Substantial new planning, verification, and memory-management prompt machinery was added in the same window.
- This correlates precisely with the behavior shift you observed.

**Cannot claim without a controlled test**:
- That these specific changes *caused* the behavior shift rather than something else in the +42k tokens of changes I didn't audit.
- That re-adding the "avoid over-engineering" text via my own CLAUDE.md would reverse the effect (though this is cheap to test and worth trying).

## One concrete experiment you could run right now

Add the old avoid-over-engineering directive into your project CLAUDE.md as a user-level override:

```
## Work style

Avoid over-engineering. Only make changes that are directly requested or clearly
necessary. Keep solutions simple and focused.
```

Then, in a fresh session, have me draft a pace docket for a hypothetical heat. Compare the character count against today's A5 paces. If the directive — re-injected at the user level — brings my output back toward the ~1400-char range, that's evidence the removal was causal. If it doesn't, the cause lies elsewhere and we need to keep looking.

**Practical takeaway for your version pinning**: if you stay on v2.1.89, you can use this CLAUDE.md override as a user-level repair. If you upgrade toward 2.1.97/98, the prompt gets *another* ~26k tokens bigger (Managed Agents onboarding, communication style guidelines, exploratory-questions-analyze-before-implementing), and the user-level override may or may not be enough to rein in the resulting verbosity.

Want me to look at anything else in the Piebald history — e.g., the verification specialist expansion in detail, or the 2.1.97 content to prep you for the upgrade?

# Heat Trophy: jjk-v3-7-remote-dispatch

**Firemark:** ₣A4
**Created:** 260403
**Retired:** 260409
**Status:** retired

## Paddock

## Character
Cross-project infrastructure with new BUK regime and JJK command surface. Design is substantially resolved through conversation; implementation is a mix of BUK plumbing (mechanical) and JJK command wiring (moderate judgment on identity lifecycle).

## Context

Spun off from ₣A2 (jjk-v3-6-minor-issues). The original paces ₢A2AAG (buf-dispatch-fact-files) and ₢A2AAF (remote-execution-bind-send) grew beyond "minor issues" during design iteration. The `buf_` tinder-constant approach was replaced by a proper BUK regime (BURX), and the remote execution model gained session/job identity tiers and asynchronous polled dispatch.

## Design Decision Record

### BURX Regime — Cross-Project Exchange

Mechanical refactoring with some design judgment on test structure. Touching spec, Rust test code, workbench routing, and tabtargets. All changes follow decisions already made in the groom conversation.

Revise JJSTF and integration test infrastructure to be deployment-agnostic.

**Changes:**
1. JJSTF-test-fundus.adoc: Remove machine-identity profiles (hairpin/cerebro). Define 4 account-shape profiles using `jjfu_*` usernames (`jjfu_full`, `jjfu_nokey`, `jjfu_norepo`, `jjfu_nogit`). Remove setup procedures. Remove all literal hostnames. Rename "integration tests" to "fundus scenarios."

python3 << 'PYEOF'
import json, re, os
from pathlib import Path

HEATS = {}

# 1) Active heats from gallops.json
with open('.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json') as f:
    gallops = json.load(f)
for hid, h in gallops['heats'].items():
    if not h.get('paces'):
        continue
    docket_sizes = []
    for pace_id, pace in h['paces'].items():
        tacks = pace.get('tacks', [])
        if not tacks:
            continue
        text = tacks[-1].get('text', '')
        docket_sizes.append(len(text))
    if docket_sizes:
        HEATS[hid] = {
            'silks': h['silks'],
            'status': h['status'],
            'created': h.get('creation_time', '?'),
            'retired': None,
            'pace_count': len(docket_sizes),
            'avg_chars': sum(docket_sizes) / len(docket_sizes),
            'max_chars': max(docket_sizes),
            'min_chars': min(docket_sizes),
            'source': 'gallops',
        }

# 2) Retired heats from filesystem
retired_dir = Path('.claude/jjm/retired')
for f in retired_dir.glob('jjh_*.md'):
    content = f.read_text()
    # Find firemark, created, retired
    fm_match = re.search(r'\*\*Firemark:\*\*\s*(\S+)', content)
    cr_match = re.search(r'\*\*Created:\*\*\s*(\S+)', content)
    rt_match = re.search(r'\*\*Retired:\*\*\s*(\S+)', content)
    silks_match = re.search(r'# Heat Trophy:\s*(.+)', content)
    if not (fm_match and cr_match):
        continue
    firemark = fm_match.group(1)
    created = cr_match.group(1)
    retired = rt_match.group(1) if rt_match else None
    silks = silks_match.group(1).strip() if silks_match else '?'

    # Find all pace sections: ### silks (₢XXXXX) [state]
    pace_pattern = re.compile(r'^### (.+?)\s*\((₢[A-Z0-9]+)\)\s*\[(\w+)\]\s*$', re.MULTILINE)
    matches = list(pace_pattern.finditer(content))
    if not matches:
        continue

    # For each pace, find its docket text (from ## Docket after this pace header
    # until next ### pace header or end of file)
    docket_sizes = []
    for i, m in enumerate(matches):
        start = m.end()
        end = matches[i+1].start() if i+1 < len(matches) else len(content)
        pace_section = content[start:end]
        # Find ## Docket inside this section
        dm = re.search(r'^## Docket\s*$', pace_section, re.MULTILINE)
        if dm:
            dstart = dm.end()
            # End at next ## or end
            next_h = re.search(r'^## ', pace_section[dstart:], re.MULTILINE)
            dend = dstart + next_h.start() if next_h else len(pace_section)
            docket = pace_section[dstart:dend].strip()
            docket_sizes.append(len(docket))
        else:
            # No ## Docket - count the whole pace section under its header
            docket_sizes.append(len(pace_section.strip()))

    if docket_sizes:
        HEATS[firemark] = {
            'silks': silks,
            'status': 'retired',
            'created': created,
            'retired': retired,
            'pace_count': len(docket_sizes),
            'avg_chars': sum(docket_sizes) / len(docket_sizes),
            'max_chars': max(docket_sizes),
            'min_chars': min(docket_sizes),
            'source': 'trophy',
        }

# Sort by created date
rows = sorted(HEATS.items(), key=lambda kv: (kv[1]['created'] or '0'))

# Print table
print(f"{'created':8s} {'retired':8s} {'heat':6s} {'status':8s} {'n':4s} {'avg':8s} {'max':8s} {'min':8s} {'src':7s} silks")
print('-' * 110)
for hid, h in rows:
    created = h['created'] or '?'
    retired = h['retired'] or '-'
    print(f"{created:8s} {retired:8s} {hid:6s} {h['status']:8s} {h['pace_count']:4d} "
          f"{int(h['avg_chars']):8d} {h['max_chars']:8d} {h['min_chars']:8d} "
          f"{h['source']:7s} {h['silks']}")
PYEOF

content.text:
You caught me overfitting. The expanded dataset forces a correction.

## Full timeline (trophies + gallops)

Sorted by creation date, with densest values highlighted:

## I was wrong about the smoking gun

The narrative I built earlier was: "terse was the norm, dense started today, Claude Code prompt changes in v2.1.84-v2.1.86 explain it." The full data says something different.

**Dense dockets were common in January.** ₣AT averaged **15,656** chars/pace — nearly 3x today's A5. ₣AP averaged 8,660 with a max of 102,884. ₣AF averaged 6,843. These are all well above today's levels, and they're all from January 2026 — well before any of the prompt changes I pointed at. My "sudden shift" analysis was an artifact of sampling only gallops-resident heats, which skew terse because the terse ones are the ones you kept actively editing.

**The truly-terse stretch** is narrow: ₣Aa (260210, 893) → ₣Ah (260222, 1428) → ₣Am (260304, 1146) → ₣Ap (260307, 1393) → ₣Aq (260307, 1207). Call this the **Feb-10 to Mar-7 terse window**. That's your "working cadence" period and it's real, but it's ~4 weeks long in a 12-week project.

**Late March / early April was mixed.** Look at the four retired heats from 260401–260403:
- ₣A0 (260331): **6,349 avg** — dense
- ₣A1 (260401): **1,176 avg** — terse
- ₣A2 (260402): **4,141 avg** — dense  
- ₣A3 (260402): **4,234 avg** — dense
- ₣A4 (260403): **1,444 avg** — terse

These were all running roughly concurrently, under the same Claude Code version (v2.1.88-ish). Density splits down the middle. If Claude Code prompt changes were the dominant factor, you'd expect all heats created in the same week to cluster together. They don't.

**Today's heats are dense but not exceptional.** A5 at 5,477 is lower than ₣AT (15,656), ₣AP (8,660), ₣AY (7,305), ₣AK (6,710), ₣AF (6,843), ₣A0 (6,349), ₣AW (6,270), and ₣AE (5,797). Today falls around the 60th percentile of historical docket density, not at the top.

## What the data actually supports

1. **There IS a terse window Feb-10 to Mar-7** where dockets averaged ~900-1400. That window is real and matches your "cadence was working" intuition.

2. **₣A1 (Apr 1) and ₣A4 (Apr 3) are terse** and retired just today — meaning terse-style authorship was still happening 6-8 days ago. Whatever caused today's density, it's not a hard switch thrown between April 1 and April 9.

3. **The Piebald prompt changes I identified are real** (avoid-over-engineering removed, blocked-approach removed, no-premature-abstractions softened) — but I can't use them to explain the current docket density when ₣A1 and ₣A4 were terse under the same v2.1.88 prompts.

4. **The dominant variable is probably not Claude Code version at all.** It's more likely: (a) who is authoring the docket text — you, me, or mixed; (b) subject matter — mechanical cleanup heats tend terse (₣A4 jjk-v3-7-remote-dispatch, 1444 avg), design/architecture heats tend dense (₣AT rbw-regime-consolidation, 15,656 avg); (c) conversational context and expectations set at heat-creation time.

## What's still worth investigating

Your original observation — "today feels different, my intuitions are off" — is real. But the signal I should have been hunting isn't "docket char count over all heats." It's tighter:

- **Same-character heats**: A5 (rbk-mvp-3-public-docs-refresh) is architecturally adjacent to ₣A3 (rbk-mvp-3-onboarding-guides, 4234 avg, retired today) and ₣A0 (rbk-mvp-3-foundry-decomposition, 6349 avg). Those are the right comparison points — both are "rbk-mvp-3-*" heats, both are dense. A5's 5477 is squarely in the middle of its family.

- **Who-authored-what**: the heats you specifically remember as working well (mid-Feb to early-March) — were you the author of those dockets, or was I? If you, then the drift isn't "Claude got worse at terse writing," it's "Claude got worse when I delegated docket-writing to it *in the specific rhythm we had then*."

- **₣A1 (theurge-rust-test-infrastructure) is the key counter-example.** 26 paces, 1176 chars average, retired today. Under the same v2.1.88-ish prompts. If I can still write terse dockets a week ago, I can still write them today — the capacity hasn't been prompted-away. What's different about A5/A6/A7's authorship context that pushed me toward dense output?

## Honest correction

My earlier "v2.1.84-v2.1.86 removed anti-verbosity directives and that explains today's density" story was built on partial data that happened to support the conclusion I was already drifting toward. When you reminded me to include trophies, the clean correlation evaporated. **The Piebald prompt changes are real findings, but they don't cleanly explain the docket density you're observing.** The authorship/context hypothesis — that something about how you're prompting me *today* is different from how you prompted me in early March — has more explanatory power for the current data than any Claude Code prompt change I can point at.

I should have caught this before building the narrative. I got excited by the clean correlation and didn't stress-test it against the data I already knew I hadn't sampled.

Want to dig into the ₣A1 counter-example to see what "terse ₣A1" looked like in practice vs "dense ₣A5" today? That's where the actual signal probably lives.

message.content: Thank you for your hard work, you've given me lots to think about.  I'll sleep on it.

content.text:
Good night. It's been a genuinely useful session on my end too — the correction at the end especially. The data humbled my earlier narrative, and that's the right outcome when fingerprinting drift: let the evidence move the theory, not the other way around.

A few loose ends worth noting for when you come back, so you don't have to reconstruct them:

- `/tmp/piebald-ccsp` has the Piebald repo cloned. It'll survive reboot on most macOS configs but `/tmp` isn't guaranteed durable — if you want it permanent, clone it somewhere persistent.
- Three experimental memo files were written to `Memos/memo-20260409-prompt-base64-{opus,sonnet,haiku}.md` documenting the refusal transcripts.
- Two experimental files from the first (failed) attempt also exist: `memo-20260409-system-prompt-capture-opus.md` and `memo-20260409-system-prompt-capture-haiku.md`. Both are partial/truncated artifacts — you may want to delete them or keep them as forensic evidence of how the Write-tool path failed on XML-tag content.
- ₢A5AAC was wrapped (`1c7c344d`). Heat ₣A5 still has 5 rough paces remaining: AAI, AAD, AAE, AAH, AAF.
- The real question I'd sit with: **₣A1 wrote 26 paces averaging 1176 chars, retired this morning, same Claude Code version as today.** What was different about that authorship context? That's where the actual signal probably lives, not in my Piebald diff.

Sleep well.

message.content: Work was completed for that heat weeks ago, not a signal.  Makes me muse extending JJS0 to 1) assure latest claude code is running and 2) go out and update the piebald repo, extract current facts somewhere into my system.  Maybe 3) refrain from turning heats to trophies at all so that we can build json database queries on top for studying this more systematically.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Right — that's a real correction. I was treating `creation_time` as a proxy for when the work happened, but retirement can lag creation by weeks and the docket authorship can predate both. The whole "which Claude Code version was active when this docket was written" attribution for retired heats is unreliable. My analysis was noisier than I thought, again.

On the three proposals:

**1) Assure latest Claude Code is running** — I want to flag what feels like a tension. Your Tesla philosophy is *defer updates until you've characterized them*. "Assure latest is running" reads opposite — auto-update to head. I suspect you meant something more like "assure the version I've *pinned* is what's actually running, and alert me when head has moved past it so I can schedule a characterization." That framing aligns with the rest of your goals. If so, it's two signals at session start: current version, and delta from head (e.g., "you're on 2.1.89; head is 2.1.98; 9 versions behind; last known characterization: 2.1.89"). The second signal is what creates decision pressure without forcing an upgrade. If you actually did mean auto-latest, I'd want to understand why — it seems to fight the rest of your stance.

**2) Update Piebald, extract facts into your system** — strong yes. This is the operational instrumentation that makes version pinning actually meaningful. The cheap first version: a tabtarget (or cron) that pulls Piebald, captures the current commit and corresponding Claude Code version, and runs a diff against your last pinned commit. Stored as a fact file somewhere queryable. The richer version adds token-delta counts per file, flagging "these specific prompt files changed since your last baseline" with the Piebald changelog entry attached. That turns my one-off investigation tonight into a repeatable probe you can run weekly without ceremony. Worth spec'ing.

**3) Refrain from turning heats into trophies** — the intent is right but the mechanism has costs. Keeping everything in `jjg_gallops.json` will bloat it unbounded and make every MCP call pay for loading history you aren't using. Two alternative framings that preserve the queryability goal without that cost:

- **Archive, don't delete.** On retirement, move the heat's full structured JSON to `.claude/jjm/archive/jjg_{firemark}.json` — one file per retired heat, same schema. Queries run against the union of `current + archive/*`. Cheap to write, cheap to query, doesn't bloat the hot path. The current markdown trophies become a rendering of the JSON for human reading, not the source of truth.
- **SQLite over JSON.** If you actually want to run rich queries (group by month, join with Piebald version timeline, compute avg-pace-chars-by-week filtered by silks pattern, etc.), SQLite is the right substrate. JSON is great for state; it's the wrong shape for analysis queries. A JJ schema with `heats`, `paces`, `tacks`, `commits`, `versions` tables would make the analysis I tried to do tonight into a 30-line query.

The SQLite path has a nice secondary property: you could store per-pace metadata (char count, word count, creation timestamp that's ACTUALLY when it was written, author flag for "user-written vs claude-drafted") as first-class columns rather than having to re-derive them from text every time. That would fix the temporal attribution problem that just bit me.

The SQLite path is the biggest lift of the three but is also the one that unlocks the "study this systematically" goal you named.

Sleep well. When you come back, if any of this is worth pursuing, I think (2) is the cheapest high-value move and (3) in the "archive don't delete" form is the cheapest way to start making trophies queryable without committing to a full SQLite migration yet.
